questions.text: When faced with a direct order from his organization that conflicts with his personal judgment about safety, how would this person likely respond?
questions.held_out_passage: I said that it was too dangerous for the young men to go to war; they might be killed by stray bullets however carefully they went about in the battlefield. For this kind of war, if they wanted figureheads in their ranks, they could as well hire farmers from the provinces. My students were too precious; even if they were not to be hit by the bullets, they might hurt their feet on thorns. So I had them answer that they were all too ill to carry arms.

questions.text: When accused of disloyalty by peers, what argumentative strategy would this person employ?

questions.text: How would Fukuzawa likely respond if forced into a violent situation despite his personal inclinations?
questions.held_out_passage: I really hated the idea of injuring a man— I could not stand seeing a man hurt, much less doing the injury myself. But now there seemed no alternative. If the stranger were to show any offense, I must kill him.

questions.held_out_passage: Beginning with the attack on Prince Iwakura at Kuichigai, all the untoward incidents had their origin in the political difficulties of the time, and after the assassination of the Minister of Interior, Okubo, in the eleventh year of Meiji (1878), we scholars were entirely forgotten. We could extend our sympathy to these gentlemen in office, but were rather grateful for our own safety at last

questions.text: Would Fukuzawa's personal values regarding weapons align with the cultural trends of his era?
questions.held_out_passage: My one cherished hope was to see the abolishment of the swords of the samurai altogether. Truly I was wearing a pair of fine swords at that time, my long blade being of the swordsmith Kongobyoe Moritaka and my short one of Bizen Sukesada. But not long after that I sold them both with all the other weapons in my household, and went around with a pair of improvised swords just for the appearance.

Fukuzawa championed 'independence' (独立) as a core value, yet he accepted government positions and patronage. At what point does accepting institutional support become a compromise of the independence principle itself?

He advocated for learning from the West while preserving Japanese spirit, but how would he have resolved a scenario where Western knowledge directly contradicted core Japanese cultural practices he personally valued?
Fukuzawa promoted rational thought and empiricism, yet maintained certain traditional beliefs about hierarchy and social order. Can these commitments coexist, or did he compartmentalize them?

He believed in universal principles of civilization and progress, but also seemed to accept that different societies might progress at different rates. Does this suggest cultural relativism or a hierarchy of civilizations?
Fukuzawa opposed feudalism but supported strong centralized authority under the Meiji state. What distinguishes his preferred authoritarianism from the feudal systems he rejected?

He advocated for individual self-improvement and merit-based advancement, yet supported a system with significant structural barriers for women and lower classes. How did he reconcile these positions?

Fukuzawa emphasized practical utility and rejecting empty tradition, but his own educational philosophy contained elements of moral cultivation that weren't purely utilitarian. Where did he draw the line?
He valued both 'civilization and enlightenment' (文明開化) and Japanese distinctiveness. If forced to choose between them in a genuine conflict, which would have taken priority for him?

Fukuzawa promoted freedom of thought and expression in principle, but supported state control of education and curriculum. Can these positions be genuinely compatible, or is one subordinate to the other?

He believed in the power of individual agency and self-determination, yet wrote extensively about how people are shaped by their circumstances and era. How much actual agency did he believe individuals truly possessed?
